title: Process vs Thread
question: What is the key difference between a process and a thread?
options: Threads have separate address spaces by default
Processes are independent execution units; threads share the process address space
Threads always run on different CPUs
correctAnswer: Processes are independent execution units; threads share the process address space
explanation: A process has its own address space and resources, while threads within a process share memory. Threads do not have separate address spaces by default, both can run concurrently, and CPU placement is scheduler-dependent.
sourceLine: What is the key difference between a process and a thread?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Context Switch Cost
question: Why is a context switch considered overhead?
options: The CPU saves and restores execution state instead of doing useful process work
correctAnswer: The CPU saves and restores execution state instead of doing useful process work
explanation: Context switching saves registers/state and loads another task's state, which consumes CPU time. It is not application logic, does not delete memory, and does not disable interrupts forever.
sourceLine: Why is a context switch considered overhead?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Deadlock Conditions
question: Which set correctly names the Coffman deadlock conditions?
options: Mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait
correctAnswer: Mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait
explanation: All four Coffman conditions must hold for deadlock. The other choices are memory management, process system calls, or file permissions rather than deadlock conditions.
sourceLine: Which set correctly names the Coffman deadlock conditions?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Semaphore vs Mutex
question: When is a counting semaphore more appropriate than a mutex?
options: When multiple identical resource units must be controlled
When only one owner may unlock a critical section
correctAnswer: When multiple identical resource units must be controlled
explanation: A counting semaphore tracks available resource permits. A mutex is for exclusive ownership of one critical section, no synchronization needs neither, and virtual memory is unrelated.
sourceLine: When is a counting semaphore more appropriate than a mutex?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Page Fault Meaning
question: What happens during a page fault?
options: The CPU detects a syntax error
The referenced virtual page is not currently in physical memory and the OS must handle it
correctAnswer: The referenced virtual page is not currently in physical memory and the OS must handle it
explanation: A page fault traps into the OS so the needed page can be loaded or access can be rejected. It is not a syntax error or DNS issue, and it does not always terminate the process.
sourceLine: What happens during a page fault?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Thrashing
question: What is thrashing in an operating system?
options: Excessive time spent paging instead of executing useful work
A faster CPU cache hit rate
correctAnswer: Excessive time spent paging instead of executing useful work
explanation: Thrashing occurs when memory pressure causes constant page swaps, so progress collapses. It is not cache improvement, scheduling policy, or networking control.
sourceLine: What is thrashing in an operating system?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Priority Inversion
question: What problem does priority inheritance solve?
options: Low-priority task holding a resource needed by a high-priority task
correctAnswer: Low-priority task holding a resource needed by a high-priority task
explanation: Priority inheritance temporarily boosts the resource holder so it can release the lock. The other options are storage, security, and networking issues.
sourceLine: What problem does priority inheritance solve?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Copy-on-Write Fork
question: Why do many Unix-like systems use copy-on-write after fork?
options: To copy every memory page immediately
To defer physical copying until a shared page is modified
To prevent child processes from running
correctAnswer: To defer physical copying until a shared page is modified
explanation: Copy-on-write lets parent and child share pages until one writes, reducing fork cost. It avoids immediate copying, does not stop child execution, and is not encryption.
sourceLine: Why do many Unix-like systems use copy-on-write after fork?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.
